key_insight: MinSurf systematically resolves which atomic surface terminations are actually stable for a given mineral facet by combining surface-energy prediction with Wulff morphology analysis.
engineering_relevance: It enables reproducible, physically representative mineral surface models for high-throughput simulations in carbon mineralization, catalysis, geo-energy storage, and related interface engineering problems.
method: Enumerate many candidate surface terminations across mineral facets.
Label surfaces with DFT and train a machine-learning interatomic potential (MinNEP).
Use Wulff construction to link predicted surface energies to equilibrium crystal morphologies.
evidence_signal: Across ten minerals, the framework built MinSurfSet with 764 slabs and 90 bulk references; MinNEP reached a 0.0119 eV/Å² MAE and about 1.14×10^4 speedup versus DFT.
watch_item: Performance depends on the coverage and quality of the enumerated training surfaces, so unusual minerals or terminations outside the sampled space may need additional DFT validation.
paper_title: MinSurf: resolving the atomic-scale stability landscape of mineral surfaces
